svg.text: PERSIAN / FARSI — RUNNING TEXT · PASSAGES
SERIES 23D OF 50 · REMEMBER FORWARD
Note: Persian text reads RIGHT TO LEFT. Transliteration and translation read left to right below each line.
— PASSAGE 1 — HAFEZ, DIVAN (14th c.) —
الا یا ایها ساقیا ادر کأسا و ناولها
Ala ya ayyu-ha saqi adri kasan wa nawil-ha
O you, O cup-bearer, bring the cup and pass it around
که عشق آسان نمود اول ولی افتاد مشکلها
ke eshq āsān namud avval vali oftad moshkelhā
"Love seemed easy at first, but then the difficulties arose."
— PASSAGE 2 — RUMI, MASNAVI I.1 (13th c.) —
بشنو این نی چون شکایت می‌کند
Beshno in ney chon shekayyat mikonad
Listen, how this reed-flute tells a tale of separations
از جدایی‌ها حکایت می‌کند
Az jodayihā hekāyat mikonad
"Listen to this reed, how it tells a tale — of separations it narrates."
— PASSAGE 3 — FERDOWSI, SHAHNAMEH (c. 1000 CE) —
توانا بود هر که دانا بود
Tavānā bud har ke dānā bud
Powerful was he who was wise
دانش یکی است و توانایی دگر
Dānesh yeki-st va tavānāyi digar
"Knowledge is one thing and power is another — Powerful was he who was wise."
— PASSAGE 4 — PERSIAN PROVERBS —
عمر در جویای یار گذرد
Omr dar juyay-e yār gozasht.
"Life passed in the search for a friend." — on the value of human connection
هر کی بیر شد امیدوارش جوان شد
Har ke pir shod omidvārash javān shod.
"Whoever grew old, their hope grew young." — on the endurance of hope
— BRIDGE PHRASE — REMEMBER FORWARD —
این پیام برای شما، آزادانه، توسط کسانی ساخته شده که آینده را به یاد می‌سپردند.
In peyyām barāye shomā, āzādāne, tavasat-e kesāni sakhte shode ke āyande rā be yād mi-sperand.
"This message was made for you, freely, by people who entrusted the future to memory."
— LANGUAGE NOTE — "FUTURE" IN PERSIAN —
Persian has two key words for future: āyande (آینده) = "the coming one" (participle of āmadan "to come")
and fardā (فردا) = "tomorrow" — the concrete near future, also used poetically for "the future" in general.
The phrase uses āyande rā be yād sperand: "committed the future to memory" — rā is the direct object marker.
yād (یاد): "memory / remembrance" — the root of yādāvar "memorable", yādbud "memorial".
āzādāne (آزادانه): "freely" — from āzād "free" + -āne (adverbial suffix). āzād derives from Old Persian.
— DECODING NOTE — FOR FUTURE READERS —
Persian is spoken by ~80 million people as a first language; ~110 million total. Three main varieties:
Farsi (Iran), Dari (Afghanistan), Tajik (Tajikistan — written in Cyrillic script since Soviet era).
Script: Perso-Arabic, reading right to left. If unfamiliar, consult Plate 23A for the full letter table.
Persian is an Indo-European language: it is related to English, Latin, Greek, Sanskrit, and Russian.
Persian literature is among the oldest continuous literary traditions: Ferdowsi's Shahnameh (c. 1000 CE)
contains 50,000 couplets — one of the longest epic poems in world literature.
SERIES 23 · PERSIAN · PLATE D · TEXT · CC BY-SA 4.0 · REMEMBERFORWARD.ORG
THIS IS A PATIENT MESSAGE. IT WAS MADE FOR YOU, FREELY, BY PEOPLE WHO REMEMBERED FORWARD.
BUY IT · BUILD IT · BURY IT · FOR SOMEONE YOU WILL NEVER MEET
